storm.summary_layperson: No thunderstorms in the area this evening — radar is completely clear and the atmosphere lacks the instability to develop storms, though isolated light showers are still possible through the afternoon.
storm.local_effect_blurb: Long Island Sound is 8°F cooler than inland air (72°F water vs. 81°F air), creating a moderate marine buffer, but no inbound storms means it isn't being tested tonight.
storm.detail_html:
Radar at 4:24 PM EDT shows no storm cells anywhere in the region — the KOKX scan detected no echoes above 35 dBZ and no cells in the approach sector. The NWS Area Forecast Discussion confirms "decreased thunderstorm potential given the lack of instability" and expects showers to diminish through the afternoon.

Atmospheric instability (CAPE) is currently 0 J/kg — no fuel for convective development. Although 0–6 km wind shear is elevated at 43 kt, shear alone cannot produce thunderstorms without an instability source. The environment is essentially inert for convection this evening.

The only active NWS alerts are two Coastal Flood Statements (minor severity) — one expiring tonight near midnight, one covering Sunday evening. Both relate to tidal flooding near the shoreline, not any severe weather threat.

Long Island Sound water temperature is 72°F against 81°F inland air — an 8°F differential producing a moderate marine stable layer over the Sound. This buffer would weaken any weak showers crossing the cove, but with no storms inbound the protective mechanism isn't being tested tonight. The protective mechanism flag is false (delta just below the 10°F trigger), so the modifier is moderate rather than strong in any case.

The overnight picture is more uncertain: HRRR extended-range shows CAPE rising to 360–870 J/kg in the f7–f12 window (roughly 10 PM–3 AM EDT), suggesting thunderstorm potential develops overnight. That window is well outside tonight's race schedule. What would change this call: a developing cell approaching from the west with CAPE recovering above 500 J/kg, or a NWS thunderstorm watch being issued — neither is present now.

wind.regime_label: Gradient-only easterly, softening through the evening
wind.tactics_blurb: ESE gradient flow gives most pressure on the left (west) side of the course, away from Greenwich Point's wind shadow — favor starboard tack on the first beat. Wind backs to NE and fades to drifter conditions by 20:00, so plan for two solid early races and treat the third as a bonus.
wind.tactics_detail_html:
Regime B-moderate (gradient-only): the 8.1°F deltaT between warm inland air and cool Sound water was adequate to support a sea breeze, but the established easterly gradient (~9 kt from airport anemometers) suppressed thermal development — only 20% of coastal stations show southerly flow, well below the sea-breeze threshold. Tonight's wind is pure synoptic gradient flow from the ESE, not a thermal.

Current RYC: instantaneous 10.4 kt at 113° ESE, 10-min average 5.6 kt, gusting 11 kt. The large gap between instantaneous and 10-min average reflects gusty, variable delivery — puffs are arriving but not sustaining. HRRR forecasts 6–7 kt at 17:00 dropping to 3 kt by 21:00, backing from E (82°) to NE (45°). This is a softening and backing synoptic gradient — wind will fade gradually, not collapse abruptly at sunset the way a thermal would. Sunset is 8:22 PM; the gradient doesn't care about it.

Direction divergence is worth noting: HRRR models 82–90° (E), but the LIS-west station ensemble median is 131° (SE) and RYC itself reads 113° (ESE). No maturation correction applies on a Regime B day. Actual race-start direction is likely ESE (110–120°) — roughly 20–30° more southerly than the raw HRRR. Lean toward the more southerly read when calling shifts at the start line.

ESE flow in the cove means partial shelter from Greenwich Point (Tod's Point) on the eastern side of the course. The west side — further from the point — will carry marginally better pressure throughout. On the beat, favor the left (west) side of the course: get on starboard tack early and work away from the Greenwich Point lee. As the breeze backs toward NE later in the evening, flow becomes more uniform across the cove and the geographic side call matters less.

Shift character: persistent backing trend (E → ENE → NE), not an oscillating sea breeze — don't play it as tactical headers, commit to the persistent lift on the left. Speed at 17:00 is 5–7 kt sustained, adequate for most one-design classes. By 19:00–19:30 sustained wind drops to ~5 kt; by 20:00 it's drifter conditions (3–4 kt). RC should plan to run two solid races by 19:30 and treat anything after that as weather-permitting. Note: HRRR gust output was not available for this run — hourly gust values above are modeled estimates based on current RYC gust-to-average ratio.

data_caveats: Buoy 44040 (Western LIS) wind observation stale — 75 min old; over-water wind is not current.
APRS N1TKS (Port Chester/Rye Brook near-W sentinel) stale — 104 min old, excluded from ensemble.
NWS forecast period is 'This Afternoon' — conditions after 20:00 are inferred from HRRR extended outlook rather than a direct NWS forecast period.
HRRR gust output not available in this fetch configuration — hourly gust values are estimated from model wind speed.
